title: भैरव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री भैरव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् ॥

प्रथमं भैरवं नाम द्वितीयं कालभैरवम्।
तृतीयं रुरुभैरवं चतुर्थं क्रोधभैरवम्॥१॥

पञ्चमं कपालमालिनं षष्ठं उन्मत्तभैरवम्।
सप्तमं भीषणं नाम अष्टमं संहारभैरवम्॥२॥

नवमं चण्डभैरवं दशमं असिताङ्गभैरवम्।
एकादशं महाभैरवं द्वादशं बटुकभैरवम्॥३॥

द्वादशैतानि नामानि भैरवस्य महात्मनः।
यः पठेत् प्रातरुत्थाय सर्वपापैः प्रमुच्यते॥४॥

चोरभयं न तस्यास्ति शत्रुभयं न विद्यते।
सर्वत्र विजयं तस्य भैरवः परिरक्षति॥५॥

॥ इति श्री भैरव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram ||

Prathamam Bhairavam Nama Dvitiyam Kala-bhairavam.
Tritiyam Ruru-bhairavam Chaturtham Krodha-bhairavam.||1||

Panchamam Kapala-malinam Shashtham Unmatta-bhairavam.
Saptamam Bhishanam Nama Ashtamam Samhara-bhairavam.||2||

Navamam Chanda-bhairavam Dashamam Asitanga-bhairavam.
Ekadasham Maha-bhairavam Dvadasham Batuka-bhairavam.||3||

Dvadashai-tani Namani Bhairavasya Mahatmanah.
Yah Pathet Pratar-utthaya Sarva-papaih Pramuchyate.||4||

Chora-bhayam Na Tasyasti Shatru-bhayam Na Vidyate.
Sarvatra Vijayam Tasya Bhairavah Parirakshati.||5||

|| Iti Shri Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: प्रथम नाम भैरव, दूसरा कालभैरव, तीसरा रुरुभैरव, चौथा क्रोधभैरव। पाँचवाँ कपालमाली, छठा उन्मत्तभैरव, सातवाँ भीषण, आठवाँ संहारभैरव। नवाँ चण्डभैरव, दसवाँ असिताङ्गभैरव, ग्यारहवाँ महाभैरव, बारहवाँ बटुकभैरव। भैरव के इन बारह नामों को प्रातःकाल उठकर जो पढ़ता है वह सभी पापों से मुक्त होता है। उसे चोरों का भय नहीं रहता, शत्रु का भय नहीं रहता, सर्वत्र विजय प्राप्त होती है।
benefits: Twelve forms of Bhairava invoked for complete protection
Classical tradition invokes this for removal of sins when recited in the morning
Traditionally invoked for removal of fear of thieves and enemies
Bhairava himself protects the devotee at all times
description: The Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram, known in Sanskrit as भैरव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्, is a devotional hymn of praise in praise of Lord Bhairava. This revered text originates from Shiva Purana. Regular recitation is believed to remove all sins when recited in the morning, twelve forms of Bhairava invoked for complete protection, and grant victory in all endeavors. For best results, devotees chant this on Saturday (Shanivar) and Tuesday (Mangalvar) and during Kalashtami and Bhairava Jayanti. Comprising 5 verses, it can be completed in approximately 3 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Recite Bhairava Dwadash Nama - 12 names of Lord Bhairava in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning for protection from enemies, thieves, and all fears.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram (भैरव द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्) is a short five-verse Sanskrit hymn enumerating the twelve sacred names (dwadash nama) of Lord Bhairava — the fierce, protective form of Shiva who is the Kshetrapala (guardian of sacred space) and Kotwal (police-chief) of Kashi and every Shakti Peetha. The twelve names recited are: (1) Bhairava, (2) Kala-Bhairava, (3) Ruru-Bhairava, (4) Krodha-Bhairava, (5) Kapala-malina, (6) Unmatta-Bhairava, (7) Bhishana, (8) Samhara-Bhairava, (9) Chanda-Bhairava, (10) Asitanga-Bhairava, (11) Maha-Bhairava, and (12) Batuka-Bhairava. The phala-shruti promises that one who recites these twelve names at dawn (pratar-utthaya) is freed from all sins, knows no fear of thieves (chora-bhaya) or enemies (shatru-bhaya), and is granted victory everywhere by Bhairava himself. (Source: classical Bhairava upasana tradition; Shiva Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Lord Bhairava and what is his connection to Shiva?
seoFaqs.answer: Lord Bhairava is the fierce, protective ugra-rupa (terrifying form) of Shiva who, according to the Shiva Purana, Brahma Vaivarta Purana and Skanda Purana, manifested from the third eye of Shiva to humble Brahma's pride and sever Brahma's fifth head — earning him the epithet Brahma-shiras-chhedaka. He is the Kotwal of Kashi (Varanasi) — no soul departs the holy city without his permission — and the Kshetrapala of all Shakti Peethas, where he stands guard with the resident Devi (e.g. Kalabhairava with Annapurna, Batuka Bhairava with Tripura Sundari). Bhairava holds a trishula, damaru, severed Brahma-skull (kapala) and noose, rides a black dog (shvana-vahana), and wears a garland of skulls (mundamala). He is the Lord of time (kala), death and the eight cardinal directions (Ashta-Bhairava). (Sources: Shiva Purana; Skanda Purana; Kashi Khanda.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the twelve names of Bhairava listed in the Dwadash Nama stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: The twelve sacred names of Bhairava listed in the stotra are: (1) Bhairava — the terrifying one, root form; (2) Kala-Bhairava — Lord of time and death, presiding deity of Kashi; (3) Ruru-Bhairava — the Bhairava of fierce dharma; (4) Krodha-Bhairava — the wrath-form; (5) Kapala-malina — wearer of the skull-garland (mundamala); (6) Unmatta-Bhairava — the intoxicated, ecstatic form; (7) Bhishana — the dreadful one who terrifies negative forces; (8) Samhara-Bhairava — the destroyer at pralaya; (9) Chanda-Bhairava — the violent one who slays evildoers; (10) Asitanga-Bhairava — the dark-limbed first of the eight Bhairavas; (11) Maha-Bhairava — the great supreme Bhairava; and (12) Batuka-Bhairava — the youthful boy-form, the most accessible and benign Bhairava worshipped for tantric initiation, removal of obstacles and child protection. Each name is a complete protective meditation. (Source: Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram be recited for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram is most powerful on Kala-Ashtami / Bhairava-Ashtami (Krishna Paksha Ashtami of every month — the day Bhairava manifested), Bhairava Jayanti (Margashirsha Krishna Ashtami — the supreme annual festival of Bhairava), Saturday (Shanivar — the day to pacify Saturn through Bhairava worship) and Tuesday (Mangalvar — the day of fierce deities). The phala-shruti specifies pratar-utthaya — recitation immediately on rising at dawn, before any food or speech, ideally facing south (Bhairava's direction). The stotra is also recited before journeys for protection from thieves and enemies, before legal cases, business deals, court appearances, and for protection of the home (vastu-shanti). Devotees perform it at the eight famous Bhairava kshetras — Kashi Vishwanath, Ujjain Mahakaleshwar, Kal Bhairav Mandir Varanasi, Patal Bhairav Ujjain. (Sources: Bhairava upasana tradition; Drikpanchang Bhairava festivals.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Bhairava Dwadash Nama Stotram grant according to tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: The phala-shruti promises the following blessings to sincere dawn recitation: (1) sarva-papaih pramuchyate — liberation from all sins, including the gravest (brahma-hatya, sura-pana, steya, guru-talpa); (2) chora-bhayam na tasyasti — complete absence of fear of thieves and burglary; (3) shatru-bhayam na vidyate — total absence of fear of enemies, court cases and conspiracies; (4) sarvatra vijayam — guaranteed victory in every undertaking, debate, conflict and competition; (5) Bhairavah parirakshati — Bhairava himself personally protects the devotee at all times; plus extended traditional benefits — (6) pacification of Shani (Saturn) and Rahu graha doshas, (7) removal of black-magic, abhichara and tantric attacks, (8) protection of the home and family from negative entities, (9) success in legal disputes, (10) protection during travel and (11) blessing for nirbhayata — fearless living. (Source: Bhairava Dwadash Nama phala-shruti.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between the Dwadash Bhairava and the Ashta Bhairava?
seoFaqs.answer: Both classifications belong to the same Bhairava-tantra tradition but enumerate different counts of the Lord's manifestations. The Ashta Bhairava (eight Bhairavas) is the older Tantric classification of Bhairava as the eight directional guardians (dik-palas) of a Shakti Peetha mandala — Asitanga (East), Ruru (South-East), Chanda (South), Krodha (South-West), Unmatta (West), Kapali (North-West), Bhishana (North) and Samhara (North-East) — each ruling a quarter and one of the eight Matrikas. The Dwadash Bhairava (twelve Bhairavas), as listed in this stotra, expands this set to twelve by adding Bhairava himself as the root, plus Kala-Bhairava (Lord of time, the Kashi Kotwal), Maha-Bhairava (the supreme), and Batuka-Bhairava (the youthful tantric initiator). The eight ring the mandala; the twelve cover the year's twelve months and the twelve directions of cosmic protection. Many tantric paddhati invoke both lists. (Sources: Rudrayamala Tantra; Bhairava upasana tradition.)
